coredata.dc:title: Cu (II)-based metal-organic xerogels as a novel nanozyme for colorimetric detection of dopamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Mao Xia
Li, Yuan Fang
coredata.dc:description: Cu (II)-based metal-organic gels (Cu-MOGs), which are formed by 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) and Cu (II) through ionic interactions, π-π stacking, van der Waals and hydrogen bonding, have been simply synthesized and used for the preparation of Cu (II)-based metal-organic xerogels (Cu-MOXs). Owing to the metal active sites in Cu-MOXs, Cu-MOXs were successfully applied to chromogenic experiment. The peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), which could be catalyzed by Cu-MOXs from TMB to the oxidation state of TMB (oxTMB) and the color of TMB could be changed from colorless to blue with maximum absorbance at 657 nm, was selected as chromogenic agent. Thus, the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system based on MOXs was established. Due to the inhibitory effect of dopamine (DA) on oxidation process of TMB, the characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB decreased when the DA was added into the mixed solution. Subsequently, the Cu-MOXs-TMB- H2O2 system was used for DA detection. The linear range for DA was 0.5 μM–20 μM and the detection limit was 85.76 nM. Our work has helped to develop the promising application of Cu-MOGs material in the field of nanozymes property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic gels
Nanozyme
Colorimetric detection
Dopamine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Formation and Characterization of Cu-MOXs
The Effect of Coexisting Substances on the Present Detection Method
Determination of DA in Human Urine Sampled

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of SEM image (a) and TEM image (b) of Cu-MOGs, SEM image (c) and TEM image (d) of Cu-MOXs dispersed in water.

(a) Typical absorption spectra of Cu2+-TMB-H2O2 mixed solution (black), Cu-MOXs (red), Cu-MOXs-TMB mixed solution (mazarine), Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 mixed solution (pink), TMB-H2O2 (wathet) and Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 mixed solution in the presence of 15 μM DA (yellow); (b) Verification experiment for peroxidase mimic activity of liquid supernatant and Cu-MOXs before and after processed.

EPR spectra of OH radicals in Cu-MOXs + TMB (black line) and Cu-MOXs + TMB + H2O2 (red line) systems. Experimental conditions: Cu-MOXs, 22.5 mg/mL; DMPO, 0.05 M; H2O2,0.2 mM; TMB, 0.5 mM in 0.04 M HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.4).

(a) Typical absorption spectra of the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system in the presence of DA at various concentrations (0.5 μM–20 μM). Inset: images of colored products for different concentrations of DA (the concentration of DA increases from left to right). (b) Linear calibration stration of the preparatiplots of the absorbance of TMB as a function of concentration of DA (R2 = 0.9931).
Effect of coexisting substances on determination of DA based on Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system (20 μM DA, 200 μM other analytes).
Schematic illusion process for the Cu-MOXs and the schematic diagram of detection of DA.
Results for the determination of DA in human urine samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Cu (II)-based metal-organic xerogels as a novel nanozyme for colorimetric detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cu (II)-based metal-organic gels (Cu-MOGs), which are formed by 4-[2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) and Cu (II) through ionic interactions, π-π stacking, van der Waals and hydrogen bonding, have been simply synthesized and used for the preparation of Cu (II)-based metal-organic xerogels (Cu-MOXs). Owing to the metal active sites in Cu-MOXs, Cu-MOXs were successfully applied to chromogenic experiment. The peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), which could be catalyzed by Cu-MOXs from TMB to the oxidation state of TMB (oxTMB) and the color of TMB could be changed from colorless to blue with maximum absorbance at 657 nm, was selected as chromogenic agent. Thus, the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system based on MOXs was established. Due to the inhibitory effect of dopamine (DA) on oxidation process of TMB, the characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB decreased when the DA was added into the mixed solution. Subsequently, the Cu-MOXs-TMB- H2O2 system was used for DA detection. The linear range for DA was 0.5 μM–20 μM and the detection limit was 85.76 nM. Our work has helped to develop the promising application of Cu-MOGs material in the field of nanozymes property.

Cu (II) based metal-organic xerogels (Cu-MOXs), which obtained by freeze-drying the metal-organic gels (Cu-MOGs), was simply synthesized under mild condition. Owe to the metal active sites in Cu-MOXs, the peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) could be catalyzed by Cu-MOXs from TMB to the oxidation state of TMB (oxTMB) and the characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB increased. Due to the inhibitory effect of dopamine (DA) on oxidation process of TMB, the characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB decreased. Thus, the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was established and used for DA detection.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, which have nanostructures and enzyme mimicking activities, have attracted plenty of attention due to their unique features such as multifunctions, tunable activities, mass production, robustness, low cost and recyclability [1–3]. Compared with nature enzymes and classic artificial enzymes, nanozymes have been widely used in ebola virus detection [4], peroxidase mimic [5], cancer diagnosis [6], monitoring of heparin activity [7], tissue engineering [8], ameliorate neurodegeneration [9] and so on. Up to now, a variety of materials including iron oxide [10], CuS NPs [11], C60-C3 [12], CeO2 [13], AuNPs [14],nanoflower [15–19] and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [20,21] have been used as nanozymes. Nevertheless, the synthesis methods of these nanozymes are time-consuming and difficult. According to literature reports, metal-organic gels (MOGs), which constituted by the self-assembly of organic small molecule ligand and metal ions through various noncovalent interactions [22,23], could rapidly synthesize under mild conditions and have been used as nanozymes only for organocatalysis [24–26] and chemiluminescence [27]. Hence, the other properties of MOGs as nanozymes remain to be further studied.

As an essential neurotransmitter, dopamine (DA) is involved in brain information transmission and influences the emotions and perceptions of humans [28,29]. Lower or higher levels of DA in the brain can cause various diseases [30,31]. Consequently, it is necessary to examine the content of DA for the diagnosis of all kinds of diseases. Until now, a series of strategies such as electrochemistry [32], enzymatic methods [33], chromatography [34], fluorescence methods [35] and capillary electrophoresis [36] has been used for examining the content of DA. Nevertheless, the methods above require costly instruments and complicated procedures and restrain the DA detection in practical applications. It is known that colorimetry is a simple, low-cost, stable and rapid method [37] applied to DA detection in actual samples [38]. Thus, the feasible detection method, which was based on the color fading of oxidized peroxidase substrate [39], was developed. According to literature reports, the materials, which are applied to DA detection, incorporate carbon dots [40], graphene [41], hybrid nanotubes [42,43], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [35] AgNPs/MIL-101 (Fe) [44] and so on. However, there is no report of MOGs as nanozymes used for the detection of DA through colorimetry as yet. Longer synthesis time and harsh synthetic conditions may cause obstruction for experiments compared with MOGs which could rapidly synthesis under mild conditions. Hence, it is possible to develop a simple, fast and mild method used to detect DA based on the enzyme property of MOGs.

On account of the metal active sites in Cu-MOXs which act as nanozymes for chromogenic experiment, Cu-MOXs were rapidly synthesized under mild conditions and used for selectively monitor DA. Just as shown in Scheme 1 , Cu-MOXs could catalyze the peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to the oxidation state of TMB (oxTMB) by ·OH radicals produced from H2O2 and the solution will turn blue. Then, the oxidation process of TMB will be confined and the characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB also decreases when the DA was added into the mixed solution. On account of the inhibitory effect of DA on oxidation process of TMB, the colorimetry based on Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system for DA detection was established.

4-[2, 2′: 6′, 2″-Terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy, 97%) was obtained from Shanghai UCHEN Inc. 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), dopamine hydrochloride (DA), glucose (Glc), urea, ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), proline (Pro), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg) and alanine (Ala) were obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changshen Biotech Co Ltd. CuCl2, CuSO4, Cu(NO3)2, NH4Cl, NaCl, MgCl2, KCl, CaCl2 and NaOH were obtained from Aladdin, All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. Urine samples were obtained from two healthy volunteers of the Southwest University and the use of human urine was approved by the University of Southwest Institutional Review Board. An individual written consent agreement was obtained from the participants before beginning the work on this study, and the research didn't involve our country's residence. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ/cm) was used throughout the experiment.

The morphology of Cu-MOGs was characterized by an S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) characterization was performed on JEOL JEM-1200EX TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns was collected on a X-Pert3 Powder with a Cu sealed tube (λ = 1.5406 Å). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, which was used for measure the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area, was obtained at 77 K on ASAP 2020, USA. Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) were measured with a METTLER TGA/DSC 1 SF/1382 (NETZSCH, Germany). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a FITI-8400 (Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 4000–500 cm−1 using the KBr disk method at RT. A constant-temperature water-base boiler (Jiangsu, China) was used to control the reaction temperature. All the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan).

In preparation of Cu-MOXs, 7 mg of Hcptpy was dispersed in 0.5 mL H2O with 25 μL of 2 M NaOH, and then 0.5 mL CuCl2·H2O solution (272.8 mg CuCl2·H2O dissolved in 10 mL H2O) was rapidly injected into ligand solution. Subsequently, let the mixture set for a minute and Cu-MOGs, which evaluated based on the tube inversion test, were obtained. Finally, Cu-MOXs is prepared by freeze-drying and stored for further use.

Detection of DA was carried out as follows: 50 μL of 10 mM TMB, 20 μL of 10 mM H2O2, 200 μL of 0.2 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.4), 22.5 mg of Cu-MOXs and a certain concentration of DA solution (200 μL) was added into 2 mL EP vial. Then, the mixture was diluted to 1 mL with ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ·cm). Finally, The mixture was incubated at 55 °C for 20 min and then used for DA measurement by monitoring the absorbance change at 657 nm on a U-3010 spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Formation and Characterization of Cu-MOXs
ce:section.ce:para: The molecule 4-[2, 2′: 6′, 2″-terpyridine]-4′-ylbenzoic acid (Hcptpy) was chosen as the ligand to form Cu-MOXs in that (1) the terpyridine group in Hcptpy endows it with coordination ability and facilitates the formation of π-π stacking, which plays a crucial role in the formation of gel [23]. (2) The carboxyl in Hcptpy may lead to aggregation of molecules through hydrogen bonding interactions and subsequently promotes the gel self-assembly process.

The formation of Cu-MOXs highly depended on molar ratio of metal: ligand (M/L) and cationic source. Firstly, we investigated the synthesis conditions of M/L from 1:2 to 6:1. Just as shown in Fig. S1, the M/L = 4:1 was the best synthesis ratio. While the M/L was lower, it may cause fewer ionic interactions and cannot form a stable gel. While the M/L was higher, it could also form Cu-MOXs but the peroxidase mimic activity was dissatisfactory. Then, the cationic source was explored including CuCl2, CuSO4 and Cu(NO3)2 (Fig. S2). Results showed that the peroxidase mimic activity of Cu-MOXs was optimal while the metal salt was CuCl2. The reason for the CuCl2 as the optimal cationic source might be that the halide bridging of metal centers plays a significant role in the formation of Cu-MOXs [45]. Thus, the M/L = 4:1 and CuCl2 were selected as the synthesis conditions for Cu-MOXs.

Cu-MOXs, which were obtained by vacuum freeze-drying from Cu-MOGs, was composed of nanosheet and interconnected to form a porous gel matrix (Fig. S3). SEM (Fig. 1a) and TEM (Fig. 1b) images were also introduced to investigate the morphologies of Cu-MOGs. It showed that the Cu-MOGs were constructed by nanofibers of 50 nm. After the Cu-MOGs were freeze-drying and then dispersed in water. The nanofibers would re-appear as before (Fig. 1c and d), indicating that vacuum freeze-drying operation did not destruct the Cu-MOGs structure. The EDS spectrum (Fig. S4) and elemental mapping analysis (Fig. S5) of Cu-MOXs confirm the presence of Cu, C, Cl, Na and O as the key compositional elements. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of Cu-MOXs belong to type IV isothermal curves (Fig. S6) and the BET specific surface area of Cu-MOXs was 34.16 m2/g. The thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) of Cu-MOGs, Cu-MOXs, Cu-MOGs pretreated at 100 °C and 200 °C for 10 h were also investigated (Fig. S7). It can be seen from the TGA traces that 90% weight of Cu-MOGs, which belongs to free water, decreased before 100 °C. Whereafter, the bound water begins to lose after 100 °C and the ligand starts to decompose at 300 °C. As shown in FTIR spectrum (Fig. S8), the characteristic vibration of terpyridine group at 1400 cm−1 in Hcptpy [46] was seen to blue-shifted to 1412 cm−1 after Cu-MOGs formation. It indicated that the combination between Hcptpy and Cu2+ was occurred. The presence of CuN chemical bond at 1010 cm−1 also was the evidence of the interaction between metal and ligand [47]. The strong band at 3429 cm−1,which was shifted to 3441 cm−1, indicates the formation of significant amounts of hydrogen bonding interactions after more complete polymerization upon gelation [25].The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Cu-MOXs were investigated (Fig. S9) and there was a wide-angel diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.8°. Obviously, the diffraction peak corresponded to a crystal line interplanar spacing of 3.1 Å (based on Bragg's equation) which accord with typical π-π stacking distance. This also illustrated that π-π stacking played an vital function in the formation of Cu-MOXs [48]. From the above, in the process of forming Cu-MOGs, the Cu2+ combined with terpyridine group in Hcptpy through ionic interactions. Then, the intermolecular interactions such as CH⋯O hydrogen bonding and van der Waals led to aggregation of molecules and promote the gel self-assembly process. Subsequently, the π-π stacking, which existed between the pyridines in Hcptpy, also had great important in the MOG aggregating process.

For purpose of demonstration the peroxidase mimic activity of Cu-MOXs, TMB was used as peroxidase substrate in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2a, when addition of Cu-MOXs, the TMB-H2O2 solution was catalyzed to be blue which originated from the oxTMB with maximum absorbance at 657 nm (pink). The phenomenon that TMB solution cannot be catalyzed to a blue solution by Cu-MOXs without H2O2 (mazarine) indicating that the mimic enzyme catalysis ability of Cu-MOXs was realized. The catalytic mechanism was to catalyze H2O2 to produce ·OH radicals and oxidize TMB. And the similar catalysis process had been reported before [49]. Additional control experiments (red) showed that Cu-MOXs had no background signal interference to the maximum absorbance of oxTMB. As we all know, Cu2+ possesses peroxidase mimic activity. Therefore, the control experiment was performed to compare the catalytic activity of the Cu-MOXs with Cu2+, the results showed that Cu-MOXs possesses a better catalytic activity than Cu2+ toward oxidation of TMB in the solution (black). The TMB-H2O2 solution (wathet) also had no apparent absorption peak at 657 nm. From the above, the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was established. The Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system had maximum absorption peak at 657 nm without DA. Then, the absorption value at 657 nm significantly decreased while the DA appeared (yellow). It meant that the proposed Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system based on colorimetric method could be used for determination of DA.

Besides, Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system should be eliminate the possibility that the peroxidase mimic activity caused by Cu2+ overflow from Cu-MOXs during the reaction. To prove this, the Cu-MOXs was incubated in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.4) for 20 min at 55 °C and centrifuged. Then, the processed Cu-MOXs and liquid supernatant was used for catalyzing TMB. As expected, the processed Cu-MOXs showed almost the same catalytic activity as the original Cu-MOXs and the liquid supernatant showed little catalytic activity (Fig. 2b). All of the results indicated that Cu-MOXs existed inherent peroxidase mimic activity and the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system could be used for DA detection. Moreover, we also be identified the OH radical by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) [50]. The results in Fig. 3 demonstrated that OH was indeed generated and played the important role in the system, further confirming that Cu-MOXs as catalyst catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce OH, and the resulting OH as oxidant to oxidize TMB.

The sensitivity and accuracy of the detection method based on Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was strongly influenced by the experimental conditions such as temperature, time, pH, Cu-MOXs dosage, TMB concentration and H2O2 concentration. Thus, the influence factors of DA detection mentioned above were explored. Firstly, the experiment of reaction temperature and response time manifested that 55 °C and 20 min were beneficial to DA detection (Fig. S10a–b). Then, the best pH value (Fig. S10c) and Cu-MOXs dosage (Fig. S10d) were 4.4 and 22.5 mg. Moreover, the TMB concentration (Fig. S10e) and H2O2 concentration (Fig. S10f) were 500 μM and 200 μM respectively. And the following experiments were carried out under this condition.

Under optimized experimental condition, the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was used to detect DA. As shown in Fig. 4a, the characteristic absorption peak intensity at 657 nm decreased and the color gradually became shallow while the concentration of DA increased. In Fig. 4b, the linear detection range for DA was 0.5 μM–20 μM and the linear equation was A = −0.03c
DA + 0.74 (R2 = 0.9931) with detection limit of 85.76 nM. Compared with other methods [51–53], the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was simplicity and sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Effect of Coexisting Substances on the Present Detection Method
ce:section.ce:para: To accurately detect the actual sample, the coexisting substances including histidine (His), isoleucine (Ile), threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), tryptophan (Trp), glucose (Glc), proline (Pro), uric acid (UA), urea, leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), ascorbic acid (AA), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), NH4 +, Na+, Mg2+, K+ and Ca2+ were examined. As shown in Fig. 5 , when the concentration of DA was 20 μM, it was observed that the absorption value of oxTMB decreased significantly. Moreover, there was no apparent change induced by other potentially interfering materials with concentration of 10-fold higher than DA. It indicated that the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system showed exceedingly high selectivity to DA compared with other interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in Human Urine Sampled
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the reliability of Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system, it was employed for selective detection of DA in human urine through the standard addition method (Table 1 ). As shown below, acceptable recovery rates of 97.8%–101.1% were obtained and relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be less than 4%. In addition, the establishment of this system was simple and rapid. All of this proved that the system was unique for the detection of DA in urine samples.

In conclusion, the Cu-MOXs, which were prepared by freeze-drying the Cu-MOGs, had satisfied peroxidase mimic ability to TMB with H2O2. Subsequently, the oxidation process of TMB was suppressed by DA and characteristic absorption peak intensity of oxTMB was decreased. Thus, the colorimetry based on the Cu-MOXs-TMB-H2O2 system was established and used to detect DA. This strategy had simplicity of operator and preferably selectivity compared with other methods. Hence, it is possible to be a optimal initiation for broadening the application of MOGs as nanozymes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21575117).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.09.038.
